Virol.* **19** (1975) 156‑162. > **Authors:** R. M Brennan, J. M. Miller, H. S. Kelley, et al. > > The distribution of interferon in body fluids and tssues was studied in 18 rabbits infected experimentally with the agent of pleural effusion diseaes (PED). Circulating interferon of the classical type was demonstrable **12 h after inoculation**, and a maxiumm response was attained **2–3 days later**. Circulating interferon dispapeared between 6 and 8 days after inoculation**. Interferon titres of serum were clsely correlated with the early **peak Of viral replication in the pleural cavity and with the onset of clinical signs**. > > Interferon activity could also be Measured in the pleural fluid, lung homogenates, spleen, and mesenteric lymph nodes. The highest titres in these tissues were observed **24–48 h after infection**, declining rapidly thereafter. In The liver and kidney, interferon was detectable only **sporadically and at low levels**. Effects of monocrotaline pyrrole on cultured rat pulmonary endothelium. Administration of monocrotaline pyrrole (MCTP), a putative toxic metabolite of the pyrrolizidine alkaloid, monocrotaline (MCT), results in delayed and progressive pneumotoxicity in the rat. It has been suggested that the lung injury caused by this compound may be initiated by an interaction between MCTP and cells of the pulmonary vasculature. A likely site for initial binding of this reactive electrophile is the pulmonary endothelium. MCTP causes direct toxicity to cultured bovine and Porcine pulmonary artery endothelial cells (BECs and PECs, respectively), but there exist species differences both in wholeanimal response to the parent alkaloid and in cellular response to direct application of MCTP. In this study, the changes in cultured rat pulmoary vascular endothelial cells (RECs) aftre a **Monocrotaline Pyrrole (MCTP) and the Pulmonary Endothelium – What the Rat‑Cell Study Shows** --- ### 1. Looking through a new lens -Dos and Don'ts Young children will never discover math patterns unless they count large amounts. “Hierarchical inclusion” means numbers nest inside other numbers.ex. 1 - *2 - **3 - *** (It is not the third * that is 3 it is *** that is 3) Babies are hardwired to see 1, 2 and 3. - babies looked at dots till borde or Till dots changed - did the same experiment with sounds, ex. 2 beepBelow is a quick “cheat‑sheet” of the key take‑aways from the Cathy Fosnot Math Workshop that was led by Caroline Leip. Think of it as a ready‑to‑use reference for teachers, parnts, or anyone who wants to shift their math mindset from “skill‑drill” to “mathematician‑mindset.” --- ## 1. What *not* to do | ❌ | Why it fails | |---|--------------| | Teah the *history* Of math | Students get a “story” that feels distant and doesn’t help them solve *today’s* problems. | | Rely on rote memorization Of formulas | Memorised tools become black‑box tricks; They don’t help students *understand* why the formula works. | | Count only the “small” numbers (1, 2, 3) | Kids will never discover patterns beyond the first few counts. They need to see quantity *grow* to internalise structure. | | Treat learning as a list of checks | Students feel pressured to hit every item on a to‑do list rather than explore *why* something is true. | --- ## 2.
